summary:
Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi (854–925 AD), known in the Latin West as Rhazes, was the preeminent physician of the [[event:2135|Islamic Golden Age]] and one of the greatest doctors in the history of medicine. A Persian polymath based largely in Baghdad and his native Ray (near modern Tehran), al-Razi authored over 200 works on medicine, philosophy, theology, and natural science — the most important of which, 'Al-Hawi' (The Comprehensive Book on Medicine), was a vast medical encyclopedia that synthesized Greek, Syriac, Persian, and Indian medical knowledge while adding al-Razi's own extensive clinical observations.

Al-Razi's contributions were revolutionary by the standards of any era. He was the first physician to distinguish clinically between smallpox and measles, writing the earliest surviving systematic description of these diseases and their differential diagnosis — a work still considered remarkable for its clinical precision. He pioneered clinical trials by testing treatments on patients and recording outcomes, applied experimental method to pharmacology, and insisted on the importance of empirical observation over theoretical authority — explicitly criticizing Galen where he believed the ancient physician to be wrong. Al-Razi also authored 'Kitab al-Mansuri,' a ten-volume medical encyclopedia dedicated to the Samanid governor of Ray, which became a standard medical text in both the Islamic world and, in Latin translation, in European universities from the 12th century onward. His work, alongside that of [[event:3614|Ibn Sina (Avicenna)]], formed the foundation of medieval medicine in both the Islamic and Christian worlds.
